title_of_original: Case 61/79 - Amministrazione delle finanze dello Stato v Denkavit italiana Srl.
decided_by: Court of Justice of the European Union
case_description: [1] Between 2004 and 2009, BElectro S.A., a Belgian importer of electronic goods, paid approximately €3.5 million in inspection charges on goods imported through the Port of Antwerp. [2] The charges were imposed under national legislation intended to cover customs-related technical inspections [3] and were applied uniformly to all electronic imports entering the country. [4] In 2011, BElectro initiated legal proceedings before a Belgian court, [5] arguing that the charges were incompatible with EU law. [6] Specifically, the company claimed that the levies constituted charges having equivalent effect to customs duties, prohibited under Article 28 and 30 TFEU, [7] and that they could not be justified as consideration for a specific service rendered. [8] The Belgian State opposed the claim, warning that retroactive reimbursement of such charges could lead to significant pressure on public finances, legal uncertainty for public authorities, [9] and unjust enrichment if the importers had passed the cost of the charges on to final consumers. [10] The court found in favor of BElectro, ruling that the inspection charges lacked a sufficient link to any individualized service [11] and therefore amounted to a breach of EU law. [12] It held that the levies were unlawful [13] and that BElectro, as an individual economic operator, was entitled to reimbursement of the sums unduly paid. [14] As a result of the ruling, in 2012 the Belgian Ministry of Finances reimbursed BElectro the full amount of €3.5 million. [15] The payment was made from the central state budget.
